data.title: Ball: Acquisitive Again, Although Small
data.description:
In September I concluded that it was time to stop kicking the can in the case of Ball Corporation (BALL). Following the divestment of the aerospace business, Ball has focused on the core aluminum can business to create more focus, although the proceeds from the divestment were quickly used to leverage up the balance sheet again after a very aggressive share buyback program.

The core aluminum can business has potential for growth, with Ball citing drivers that favor aluminum over glass and plastics to preserve and consume food and drinks. That is a long-term driver, but in reality, Ball is navigating a complex world in which consumer packaged goods volumes are pressured, tariffs have been introduced, and commodity prices are volatile.

All About Aluminum

Ball is a producer and distributor of aluminum cans, which are used in a wide range of consumer packaging applications, predominantly used to preserve food and drinks. Besides better preservation of quality and taste, aluminum has major recycling benefits as well, including lower melting temperatures and no degradation amidst its recycling process. Pre-2023, the company had a substantial aerospace business as well, activities sold in a $5.6 billion deal in 2023.

After that divestment, Ball became a $12 billion pure play on the production and sale of aluminum cans, employing about 16,000 people who are active across the globe. About half of these sales are generated in North & Central America, complemented by EMEA operations, which make up about a third of sales, with the remainder of revenues generated in South America.

Earlier this year, Ball reported its results for 2024, with sales down 2% to $11.8 billion. Adjusted earnings were reported at $3.17 per share, up twenty-seven cents from the year before. Net debt of $4.8 billion translates into a 2.5 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA reported around $2 billion, as much of the aerospace proceeds were used to buy back substantial amounts of stock.

The company reported a strong 8% increase in first quarter sales, driven by tariffs, with adjusted earnings up eight cents to $0.76 per share. Second quarter sales rose as much as 13%, with adjusted earnings up sixteen cents to $0.90 per share. At this trend and trajectory, earnings were seen around $3.50 per share as I called the composition of growth poor, driven by buybacks, which actually pushed up net debt to $6.7 billion for a leverage ratio in the mid-3s, with much of the money used for buybacks (at higher levels).

With 277 million shares trading at $49 in September, the company's equity was valued at $13.5 billion, for a $20 billion enterprise valuation. That substantial net debt load would come down a bit following the sale of a 41% stake in Ball United Arab Cab Manufacturing, with Ball obtaining $70 million in proceeds in connection with the sale.

I had some reservations about the capital allocation moves, following the aggressive buybacks in recent times, after the company actually diluted the shareholder base in the late 2010s to make acquisitions. Trading at around 10 times EBITDA, the highly leveraged business saw its equity trade at around 14 times earnings here, which is a modest multiple, yet this comes amidst an uneven track record and much debt employed (again).

Despite this, the cheapness and predictability of the business made it so that a small position could be justified, as I did not expect to update the thesis so soon, after Ball pursued M&A again, although in all honesty, it has announced a rather small deal here.

What Is Happening?

Since September, shares of Ball have been trading pretty flat at levels in the high forties. Early in November, the company reported its third-quarter results. Quarterly revenues rose by nearly 10% to $3.38 billion, aided by higher aluminum prices. Adjusted earnings of $1.02 per share were up some eleven cents on the year before, reported at $2.66 per share so far this year, making a $3.50 per share number look very attainable for the year.

Net debt was reported at $6.64 billion, for a 3.3 times leverage ratio, as net debt remained stable, despite some continued buybacks, which reduced the share count to 273 million shares, now valuing equity at $13.0 billion at $47.50 per share, for a $19.6 billion enterprise valuation. At this rate, Ball is firmly on track to generate about $13 billion in sales, with operating earnings seen around 10% of sales and earnings multiples seen in the lower to mid-teens.

With quite some debt employed and rather aggressive capital allocation decisions undertaken, I am a bit surprised to see that Ball is announcing its next deal. Early in December, Ball announced that it had reached an agreement to acquire an 80% stake in Benepack, a European can manufacturer with operations in Belgium and Hungary.

The purchase comes at a EUR 184 million payment, or about $210 million, based on prevailing currency rates. This values the transaction at about 1 percent of the current valuation, as unfortunately no financial details on the purchase have been announced.

Still Upbeat

With shares trading flat and continued progress made in the third quarter, while the next bolt-on deal is announced, I remain upbeat on the shares here given a great earnings yield, as leverage is a bit high to my taste.

The deal in Europe is really a bolt-on one but tells us little about the pro forma implications, but likely these come at reasonable valuations. Some quick searches online reveal just over $100 million in revenue contribution, as that seems largely in line, or at a small premium, to Ball's sales multiples here.

Given all this, I remain constructive here but do not expect major and spectacular developments in the business or its share price meanwhile. However, a continued set-up of solid earnings yield and continued buybacks makes the risk-reward and expected returns from here seem reasonably compelling in my view, making me continue to hold onto the shares, anticipating a partial re-rating down the road.

data.title: The 'Edge AI' Bear Case
data.description:
On “Invest Like The Best” podcast, Gavin Baker mentioned the following:

“In three years, on a bigger phone, you’ll be able to run a pruned-down version of Gemini 5, Grok 4, or ChatGPT. And that’s free. This is clearly Apple’s strategy - we’re going to make it privacy-safe and run on the phone. Other than scaling laws breaking, edge AI is by far the most plausible and scariest bear case.”

One of my hypotheses in investing in general is most bear cases never truly die; they just keep re-appearing time and again and most investors start paying more attention to these perpetual bear cases only when stock prices force them to, i.e., when stocks go down a lot. To Baker’s credit, he’s highlighting a risk when stocks are actually doing fine.

But indeed, edge AI has been touted as a plausible bear case for the “AI trade” for much of the last couple of years. Nilesh Jasani was one of the investors who actually took this bear case seriously and explained the appeal of edge AI:

“In late 2023 and early 2024, the promise of edge computing was not a niche technical idea; it was a palpable hum of excitement. The logic was clean and compelling. Moving artificial intelligence to the edge would solve the technology’s most pressing problems.First, there was privacy. Processing data on your own device meant it never had to travel to a server owned by someone else. Your secrets would remain your own. Second was latency or annoying delays. With the thinking done locally, responses would be instantaneous, a crucial feature for everything from conversations with voice assistants and augmented reality to autonomous vehicles. Finally, there was cost and access. Why rent time on a remote supercomputer when your own device could do the work? This would democratize AI, making it reliable even without a perfect internet connection.”

However, Jasani admitted that it was “wrong.” The reality didn’t match edge AI’s promises at all. From his piece again:

“Top-tier smartphones like Apple’s iPhone 16 and Samsung’s latest Galaxy look and work pretty much like the models from a few years prior. Yes, they tout AI-powered camera modes and smarter assistants, but there’s little truly new that runs independently on the device. All the major phone launches emphasized AI features, yet those features often amounted to things like slightly better autocorrect, AI image editing, or voice dictation – useful, sure, but hardly the seismic shift envisioned by edge computing evangelists.Notably, many of these “AI features” still lean on the cloud. Apple’s vaunted Apple Intelligence gave iPhones some on-device smarts, but even Apple admitted that for more “sophisticated commands” the system quietly reaches out for “help from ChatGPT” or other cloud models. In other words, your iPhone might rewrite a text message offline, but if you ask it to compose a complex email or summarize a document, it’s likely pinging a server somewhere. Apple’s commitment to privacy means those requests are anonymized and encrypted, but they’re not happening on your phone’s CPU alone.”

I recently bought an “AI PC,” and the salesperson at “Best Buy” was passionately making the case that conversations on CoPilot stays on device and not on the cloud. That’s not true. So, there is clearly a gulf of difference between what edge AI has promised so far and how much they could make it work.

But is it just a timing mismatch, and much of the promises of edge AI will eventually bear fruit? If edge AI becomes reality in the medium to long term, the biggest losers are likely to be hyperscalers who are hoping to generate billions of inference revenue on their ongoing capex spree.

Since they are Nvidia’s (NVDA) biggest customers today, you might imagine Jensen Huang to be highly incentivized to not believe in edge AI’s promises. Interestingly, Huang himself, in his recent Joe Rogan podcast appearance, made the case that edge AI is inevitable:

“the fact of the matter is your phone’s going to run AI just fine all by itself in a few years. Today, it already does it fairly decently. And so the fact that every country, every nation, every society will have the benefit of very good AI. It might not be tomorrow’s AI. It might be yesterday’s AI, but yesterday’s AI is freaking amazing. You know, in 10 years time, 9-year-old AI is going to be amazing. You don’t need 10-year old AI. You don’t need frontier AI…we need frontier AI because we want to be the world leader. But for every single country, everybody, I think the capability to elevate everybody’s knowledge and capability and intelligence, that day is coming.”

If edge AI starts to deliver on its promises, I wonder if it’s an underappreciated headwind for OpenAI (OPENAI). Following the GPT-5 backlash, Sam Altman once tweeted, “the percentage of users using reasoning models each day is significantly increasing; for example, for free users we went from <1% to 7%, and for plus users from 7% to 24%.”

It was a bit surprising to me that the vast majority of ChatGPT users are not using reasoning models. If your queries can be responded to without reasoning, those are exactly the type of queries that can be easily done by on device models in a few years.

And if the free model on your device can have private, uninterrupted (personality won’t change due to any model changes) conversations, would ChatGPT remain compelling enough for incremental users to pay for subscription?

There is indeed a real possibility that “System 1” (fast/reflexive) queries are just commodities and it is the “System 2” (deep thinking) queries that prove to be high-value ones. It may be instructive to think about why Anthropic (ANTHRO) is almost solely focusing on “System 2” queries, whereas ChatGPT’s vast majority of queries are still likely to be “System 1” queries.

System 1 queries can be quite profitable (very low inference costs now, which may even be almost zero in an edge AI scenario), but it is also going to be very hard to convince users that it is worth paying a subscription for in a few years.

Of course, we cannot quite outline the impact of certain risks playing out, imagining ceteris paribus. Sam Altman has already declared “code red” in the face of Gemini’s recent growth spurt, and OpenAI will certainly not sit idly if edge AI ever starts proving to be a headwind for user growth.

Let’s not forget that OpenAI still has the “Midas touch” when it comes to its product. They may be behind on model quality, they may be less focused than Anthropic, and they clearly don’t have the infrastructure advantage that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) does, but AI still is synonymous with “ChatGPT” for most people. That itself will likely buy them time, but the clock is ticking.

data.title: AMG Yacktman Focused Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

  • ► The AMG Yacktman Focused Fund (the Fund) returned 4.98% for the third quarter of 2025, underperforming the Russell 1000 ® Value Index, which returned 5.33%, and underperforming the S&P 500 ® Index, which returned 8.12%. For the year, the Fund has outperformed the Russell 1000 ® Value Index and slightly underperformed the S&P 500 Index.
  • ► The S&P 500 Index, after posting mid-20 percent returns in both 2023 and 2024, has returned to all-time highs with many quality companies trading at extremely expensive valuations. The market remains momentum-driven with the greatest predictor of share price winners being companies with rising share prices. We believe the environment is full of risks—geopolitical, regulatory, leverage, supply chain, and tariffs, to name a few. At Yacktman, we constantly consider “what could go wrong” and evaluate companies and their business performance across economic cycles. We carefully consider the price that we pay for an investment, and we seek opportunities where the value of the company is misunderstood and where the price we pay fairly compensates us for the risks.
  • ► We remember when Steve Yacktman coined the phrase “good train day,” in late 2000. Steve was commuting on the Northwest Line of the Metra train when Yacktman was headquartered in Chicago. Passengers commuted to and from downtown Chicago and usually sat in the same seats of the same car on the way home. Jason Subotky was living in the Bay Area at the time. Steve and Jason would talk on the phone during Steve's commute at the end of each day. The market initially peaked in March, then it rebounded in the fall from its initial “correction” until it cracked in late 2000. Steve would say to Jason, “yeah, it is a good train day” as the market fell. Steve does not have the quietest of voices. Day after day, those words became more and more unwelcome as fellow commuters' dot-com returns were unwinding and continued to correct until late 2002. Subsequently, Yacktman moved its offices to Austin.
  • Following the dot-com bubble, the S&P 500 was essentially flat over the 2000-2009 time period. Yacktman annualized double-digit returns over that decade. These environments with tumultuous markets have been when Yacktman produces its best results for investors. We believe that the portfolio is well positioned to deliver strong risk-adjusted returns.

Top Contributors/Detractors

  • Samsung has been a solid contributor to performance after having detracted from results last year. The company announced its first design win with Nvidia (NVDA) for high bandwidth memory (HBM) chips this quarter. Samsung was late to the artificial intelligence (AI) party, and this milestone validates its position in the HBM chip market. Memory chips can be found in a vast array of end products, from data center servers to automobiles and refrigerators. There is a finite production capacity for dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) and a small number of players who can provide these components. Samsung remains a leader in semiconductor fabrication and benefits from its locations that are not in Taiwan (South Korea and Texas). Samsung is also enjoying tailwinds from the continued regulatory improvements in South Korea, a trend that we believe will continue to unfold as governance and market access improve. Other contributors for the quarter include Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) and Fox. Alphabet has participated in the AI frenzy despite the longer-term risks to its advertising revenue. Fox benefited from the Murdoch family resolving its disputes and removing the ambiguity of succession. Lachland Murdoch will take the reins, removing the risk of voting control split between the four siblings.
  • Bolloré was a detractor for the quarter but the underlying thesis is very much intact. Just a few years ago, Bolloré was a complicated conglomerate that had high leverage, an African port and logistics company, a European freight forwarding company, ownership in Vivendi SE (VIVHY), and ownership in Universal Music Group N.V. (UMGNF) Today, the company has simplified its holdings to Vivendi and Universal Music Group plus significant net cash on the balance sheet from the sale of non-media assets. Universal Music group continues to be a strong component of value, and it is expected to be listed in the U.S. later this year or early next year. We believe that Bolloré's net asset value (NAV) is worth mid-teens, and with the current share price in the mid-single digits, the investment represents an extremely attractive value. Meanwhile, the company continues to make moves to further simplify its structure.
  • U-Haul also detracted from performance for the quarter and for the year. The company continues to invest its cashflow to build out its self-storage business. Self-storage real estate investment trust (REIT) analysts are accustomed to evaluating the economics of this part of the business with initial capital investment followed by low occupancy until a location matures and produces strong recurring revenue at higher occupancy. Because U-Haul also has a truck rental business, the company does not cleanly fit into a sector category, and it is not widely followed by analysts. We appreciate the disciplined capital allocation that U-Haul's leaders continue to demonstrate.

Outlook

  • One of the potential benefits of managing a strategy for over 30 years is the experience of managing capital through the dot-com bubble, the telecom bubble, the global financial crisis, and the global pandemic. No one can see a market bubble until it corrects, but when stock prices have become too expensive and they fall, most investors suddenly become focused on risk. Yacktman is constantly focused on risks, and we have constructed a portfolio of companies that we believe is well-positioned to protect capital and to offer a differentiated returns stream with active management. We look forward to another set of "good train days."

data.title: Moving From Cuts To Caution: Fed Enters 2026 In Wait-And-See Mode
data.description:
By Tiffany Wilding and Allison Boxer

With the policy rate in neutral territory, the Fed embraces data dependence, and faces a delicate balancing act in 2026.

The Federal Reserve delivered a widely expected 25 basis point (bp) rate cut in December, then signaled a more data-dependent path ahead. Barring an economic shock, we probably won’t see another rate cut until the second half of next year.

In the weeks since the previous meeting in October, several Fed officials had expressed discomfort with further rate cuts. Indeed, two officials dissented outright at this meeting by voting to hold the rate steady (and one official voted to cut 50 bps).

Four officials used the new economic projections – or “dot plot” – to show they would have preferred to pause in December. Consistent with this sentiment, the statement changes reflected a committee poised to hold rates steady after cutting rates by 75 bps over the past three meetings.

During the press conference, Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasized downside labor market risks as an impetus for the rate cut. Given an increasingly large share of the rate-setting committee seemed uncomfortable with further cuts, and with the policy rate within “plausible estimates of neutral,” he noted that the Fed should be well-positioned to wait for more data and to react as risks to the outlook evolve.

Bond yields moved modestly lower as Powell declined to provide a firmer signal that the Fed does not expect to cut again and instead embraced data dependence.

The fed funds rate in 2026

Our policy outlook is largely aligned with that of Fed officials and with current market pricing: We expect the Fed to hold rates steady in a range of 3.5%–3.75% for the remainder of Powell’s term as chair, which runs through May, before resuming gradual rate cuts later in the year under new Fed leadership.

There’s “persistent tension” in both parts of the dual mandate, as Powell phrased it. Surprisingly resilient U.S. growth in the second half of 2025, plus household and business tax cuts that will boost after-tax incomes in 2026, risks further delaying inflation’s progress toward the Fed’s 2% goal.

Nevertheless, the Fed remains well-placed to cut further if stronger growth fails to stabilize the increasingly soft U.S. labor market. And even if downside risks are avoided, we believe the Fed will likely be able to resume gradual rate cuts in late 2026 as inflationary pressures ease.

The Fed faces a delicate balancing act: curb inflation while supporting the labor market so that households feel economically secure. Powell warned there is no risk-free path. He noted a reasonable baseline is that tariff-driven inflation effects – essentially a one-time shift in the price level – will likely ease, and he highlighted notable progress this year on non-tariff-related inflation.

On the labor market, October data were not collected, and November data are incomplete, so upcoming data must be assessed carefully. Consumer spending and productivity appear to support economic activity. Fiscal policy remains supportive, and business investment in AI is holding up.

December statement and projections: Policy rate is within the range of neutral

The Fed statement had just one notable change. It borrowed a phrase from the December 2024 statement – when the Fed likewise had just cut by 75 bps over the course of three meetings – by saying that the “extent and timing” of further rate adjustments will depend on the data. And after the December 2024 statement, the Fed held the rate steady for much of 2025.

Similarly, the Fed’s new economic projections were only modestly changed versus their September vintage. Growth forecasts for 2026 were revised higher, with the median participant now projecting 2.3% versus 1.8% before. But this notable uptick didn’t translate into large changes elsewhere.

Median unemployment projections were unchanged, and inflation projections were just barely revised down. The median rate path projection was unchanged, implying only one rate cut in 2026.

The December statement and projections reflect a Fed that now views monetary policy as within the range of neutral estimates – i.e., a rate the Fed expects will help promote trend-like growth.

Technical changes to the Fed balance sheet

The Fed also announced technical adjustments to its balance sheet and repo programs to address recent volatility in money market rates. The Fed will start Treasury bill purchases to help provide liquidity and ensure that reserve balances do not decline further.

This announcement was both somewhat earlier and somewhat larger in size than many market participants were expecting, and we saw money market rates rally a few basis points on the news.

Powell went out of his way in the press conference to emphasize that this was a technical adjustment that should not be confused with the kind of policy-focused quantitative easing programs the Fed undertook in an effort to stabilize the economy in the past two recessions.

data.title: Fidelity Freedom 2035 Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update and Portfolio Review

  • Global equities rallied in Q3 amid a constructive expansionary backdrop and strong corporate fundamentals. U.S. corporate earnings revisions accelerated upward across most sectors. After pausing since its December 2024 rate cut, the Federal Reserve cut its short-term policy rate by 25 basis points on September 17, citing that weaker labor markets outweighed the risk of above-target inflation. Inflation remained above target, with goods prices moving upward for the first time in three years. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury bond yields finished Q3 slightly lower than at midyear amid softer labor-market data, Fed easing, sticky inflation and medium-term fiscal challenges.

  • Active asset allocation positioning contributed to the fund's performance versus the Composite index in Q3, whereas security selection among the underlying funds detracted. An overweight in emerging-markets equities was the largest contributor to the fund's relative result, as the region outperformed all others during the quarter. An underweight in investment-grade bonds also contributed versus the Composite.
  • The impact of active security selection varied by asset class. Non-U.S. equity managers underperformed, in aggregate, whereas the strong performance of U.S. large-cap growth managers contributed. Active management within fixed income also helped the fund's relative result.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

  • Large-cap U.S. equity growth investments notably contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter. The largest fund-level contributors were:
    • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund (FCGSX) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the information technology sector contributed to the fund's relative performance in the quarter, especially out-of-benchmark positions in technology hardware and equipment companies Sandisk (SNDK) and Ciena (CIEN), and a larger-than-benchmark stake in Pure Storage (PSTG). Overweight positions in semiconductor companies Nvidia (NVDA) and Astera Labs (ALAB) also added relative value.

Relative Detractor (representative across vintages):

  • The largest fund-level detractors:
    • Fidelity® Series Overseas Fund (FSOSX) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index. The fund is managed by Vincent Montemaggiore, who is valuation sensitive and focuses on higher-quality companies with durable growth. Security selection within the financials and industrials sectors detracted from performance this quarter. Within financial services, overweight positions in Deutsche Boerse and London Stock Exchange Group (LSEGY) were meaningful individual relative detractors. An out-of-benchmark position in software firm Constellation Software (CSU:CA) was the largest relative detractor.
    • Fidelity® Series Canada Fund (FCNSX) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Canada Index. The fund is managed by Ryan Oldham, who targets companies with a high and stable return, and high payback to shareholders (through elevated dividends and share buybacks), which he believes can outperform over the long-term if purchased at attractive valuations. Security selection within information technology, financials and materials detracted for the quarter. Not owning positions in materials companies and benchmark components Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Barrick Mining (B), as well as a larger-than-benchmark stake in Constellation Software, were the largest individual relative detractors.

Active Asset Allocation:

  • Active asset allocation positioning added value for the fund relative to its Composite index this quarter, whereas security selection detracted.

Outlook and Positioning

  • Target-date funds are long-term strategies designed to help keep participants on track toward their retirement goals through different market environments. Strategic asset allocation decisions are focused on providing age-appropriate asset allocation throughout participants' changing time horizons.
  • Based on our long-term views of participant needs and capital markets, we have updated the glide path and strategic asset allocation of the strategies. We have increased exposure to equities for early-career investors, and increased exposure to both equities and inflation-sensitive assets for investors near retirement or in retirement. We believe these updates can improve the likelihood that participants sustain income through later ages, while also maintaining diversification to limit the potential for significant or extended drawdowns.
  • As of September 30, we believe trends in the economy and policy conditions may imply a regime change for financial markets. In Q3, high debt, aging demographics, peak globalization and geopolitics continued to create uncertainty in the path of inflation, policy and corporate profits.
  • The fund's active allocation positioning reflects our intermediate-term view of assets that we believe are mispriced relative to our estimate of fair value.
  • Looking ahead, we expect the U.S. economy to remain in expansion, supported by strong corporate fundamentals and monetary and fiscal easing. Outside the U.S., countries have exhibited various policies to support domestic growth. We maintain an overweight allocation to equities.
  • We believe non-U.S. equities remain attractively valued relative to U.S. equities, and may benefit from continued dollar weakness, while earnings revisions are accelerating in the U.S. As a result, we maintain an overweight in non-U.S. equities but increased U.S. equity exposure the past three months.
  • With services inflation no longer falling and goods prices rising due to tariffs, we expect inflation to remain around 3% next year, higher than the Fed's 2% target. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities largely reflect this view, so the fund is positioned with Composite-neutral exposure to inflation-sensitive assets.

Investment Approach

Fidelity Freedom® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire.

Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation.

Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Income Fund.

The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.

STRATEGIC GLIDE PATH

■ Current Year

The allocation is conservative in the early years (Years to Retirement 50+), with a high allocation to U.S. Equity Funds (around 55%) and a low allocation to Short-Term Funds (around 5%). After the target date (Years in Retirement 0-5), the allocation becomes increasingly conservative, with a high allocation to Bond Funds (around 80%) and a low allocation to U.S. Equity Funds (around 10%).

Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bills Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index of investment-grade, fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with remaining maturities from 3 up to (but not including) 6 months, excluding zero coupon strips.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

There are multiple drivers of performance for the Funds. Strategic asset allocation is expected to be the primary source of returns over long-term periods, while incremental excess return can result from active asset allocation and building-block manager decisions. Strategic allocation decisions are based on research and insights related to the factors that influence outcomes over long-term periods, including diversification, capital markets and participant behaviors. We periodically increase the diversification of our strategic asset allocation in an attempt to better navigate capital market uncertainty. Active asset allocation is intended to provide shareholders with exposure to asset classes that are mispriced and may offer additional returns and opportunities for risk mitigation. Active allocation expands the investment opportunity set and provides flexibility to adjust asset class exposures over intermediate-term periods. Allocations to active building-block managers offer shareholders the opportunity to benefit from informational advantages across Fidelity's asset management platform.

Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: PGIM Core Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • The bull market for fixed income continued through Q3 2025. U.S. Treasury yields declined and the curve steepened over the quarter as the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 25 basis points (BPS) at its September meeting. Meanwhile, credit products generally posted strong total returns, with spreads for investment grade corporates near multi-decade tights and high yield spreads continuing to tighten amid persistently strong technicals and stable fundamentals. Securitized credit spreads also tightened, with high-quality commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) both tightening over the quarter. Emerging market hard currency, local rates, and corporates also posted strong total returns.
  • The global economy continues to demonstrate resilience despite significant shifts in U.S. trade and investment policies as well as their accompanying uncertainties. While the impact of higher tariffs and changing trade flows is beginning to show in price and production data, consumer spending, labor markets, and corporate performance remain mostly robust, providing a critical buffer. As a result, our base case for major economies remains one of “muddle through,” that is moderate growth and inflation, even amid heightened policy uncertainty.
  • Developed market rate complexes received some direction in Q3 as the Fed resumed what could become a prolonged, and possibly deep, rate-cutting cycle. In Europe, inflation remains subdued, and we believe the European Central Bank (ECB) has largely concluded its easing cycle, with only one cut expected in 2026 as insurance against downside risks. While other Western banks are generally on hold or likely to cut in the quarters ahead, the Bank of Japan (BoJ) is the lone major central bank on a rate-hiking trajectory.
  • We maintain that monetary policy divergence, combined with growth convergence among major economies, points to sustained U.S. dollar weakness. That said, much of this adjustment has already occurred, and future direction hinges on U.S. policy choices. Stronger emerging market (EM) currencies and contained commodity prices will limit upside price pressures, allowing EM central banks to cut rates more (though the picture remains diverse).
  • While “muddle through” remains our base case, the key risk lies in an overheating U.S. economy fueled by a more dovish Fed, alongside looser fiscal policy. Our baseline assumes the Fed cuts its policy rate to 3.25%, staying “on the higher side of neutral,” consistent with Chair Powell’s guidance. However, if the Fed downplays tariff-driven inflation beyond initial effects and prioritizes downside risks, particularly in labor markets, it could cut rates more aggressively than anticipated. This would deliver an additional stimulus, giving markets and the global economy a short-lived “sugar high.”
  • In contrast to the broken yield curve dynamics in H1—when short-term yields fell while long-term yields rose—things have changed in the home stretch of 2025. Yes, yield curve steepening seems likely to continue. But long yields are now moving along with short rates and clearly seem past their peaks in the U.S., which carries bullish implications for other Western markets given the influence of the U.S. market on cross-market correlations.
  • Distribution tails are thick, not only on the downside in a world of fraught geopolitics and trade tensions, but similarly on the upside if a Fed capture scenario ensues—i.e., possibly positive over the near to intermediate term, but risky in the longer term. Hence, we remain vigilant. Yet, these potential developments are lower-probability scenarios, and, ultimately, we are guardedly optimistic regarding our base case—global muddle through scenarios—which should be well enough for the fixed income markets.
  • This environment continues to challenge investors and analysts alike. Similarly, it continues to create distortions in the interest rate, sector, issuer, and currency realms, resulting in risks, but also continued opportunities for adding value through active management across the public and private asset spectrum.
  • The 2022 selloff left yields at respectable levels—which largely remain intact—with an unusual net result: a potentially long-lived bull market primarily driven by the accretion of income, rather than a short bull market fueled by a quick drop in yields. The trend of compressing credit spreads should generally continue, albeit more modestly given the tight spread levels. These conditions describe “a carry market” if there ever was one.

3Q 2025 Performance

  • The Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, on a gross basis.
  • Overall sector allocation was the largest contributor to performance during the period, with overweights to the non-agency CMBS AAA, CLO AAA, and mortgage-backed securities (MBS) sectors, along with an overweight to the U.S. investment grade corporate sector, contributing the most. This was partially offset by an overweight to the U.S. high yield sector, which was the largest detractor.
  • Overall security selection also contributed performance, with selection in U.S. investment grade corporates, non-agency CMBS AAA, asset-backed securities (ABS), and non-agency MBS contributing the most.
  • Within credit, positioning in technology was the largest contributor to performance.
  • The Fund's duration positioning contributed to performance as front-end U.S. Treasury yields fell. The Fund's yield curve positioning detracted.
  • From a market perspective, having more risk in the Fund, on average, than the relevant benchmark over the period contributed to performance.

Top-Down Macro Positioning

Duration (Current: 5.9 yrs, Previous Quarter: 5.9 yrs)

  • A cooling U.S. labor market, higher tariffs, and the associated macro uncertainty weigh on purchasing power, investment, and ultimately on U.S. economic activity. Our base case in the U.S. is for weaker-but-positive growth. We see the balance of risks as slightly skewed to the upside. Our main risk scenario ("Overheating") is one where the Fed cuts rates by more than justified by economic fundamentals thus engineering a rise in activity growth and inflation. A "productivity boost" is also an upside risk, while recession and mild stagflation are downside risks.
  • Despite the arrival of an aggressively dovish Fed Governor, the Fed's most recent 25-bp rate cut is likely the first in what continues to look like a fairly gradual path towards neutral, which we estimate to be in the 3.0% area. We expect two more 25-bp rate cuts by year end, which would bring the Fed funds rate to 3.625%.
  • Looking further ahead, Chair Powell's term will conclude in May 2026, likely leading to another dovish adjustment to the Fed board. At this point, the market is pricing in a Fed funds rate of 3.0% by the end of 2026. A push into accommodative territory is likely to require a combination of a more dovish Fed voting slate and/or a significant slowing in growth and/or inflation.

Curve Positioning

  • In contrast to the broken yield curve dynamics in H1—when short-term U.S. Treasury yields fell while long-term yields rose—things have changed in the home stretch of 2025. Yes, yield curve steepening seems likely to continue. But long yields are now moving along with short rates and clearly seem past their peaks in the U.S. This carries bullish implications for other Western markets given the influence of the U.S. market on cross-market correlations.
  • Steeper yield curves should remain prominent globally. The long end of developed market sovereign curves have become more sensitive to politics and fiscal news. This has led to sporadic "term premium" shocks. Two different camps are emerging: 1) Governments that struggle to bring public debt down despite some efforts to do so (e.g. France and the UK), and 2) those that want to use fiscal policy more actively to support growth (e.g., Germany, Japan, and the U.S.).
  • Market pricing around an ECB rate cut next year remains marginal, yet we see two factors potentially supporting an additional 25-bp reduction in the first half of 2026. Further euro appreciation vs. U.S. dollar could contribute to more moderate growth and inflation, and the ECB may also seek to prevent any temporary below-target inflation that is expected in 2026 from becoming entrenched.

Bottom-Up Sector Positioning

  • Credit products generally posted strong total returns, with spreads for investment grade corporates near multi-decade tights and high yield spreads continuing to tighten amid persistently strong technicals and stable fundamentals. Securitized credit spreads also tightened, with high-quality CMBS and CLOs both tightening over the quarter. Emerging market hard currency, local rates, and corporates also posted strong total returns.
  • We expect fundamental trends to vary by industry. In addition, we remain watchful of more cyclical and consumer discretionary sectors, as well as those that might be disproportionately impacted by tariffs.
  • At this stage in the credit cycle, event risks, bifurcated consumers preferences, and moderating credit metrics (along with increased distressed exchanges), point our allocations towards high-quality securities. We remain overweight an array of credit sectors including both investment-grade and high yield corporates, high-quality structured products, and emerging markets.

Corporate Credit

Investment-Grade Corporates (Current: 26.3%, Previous Quarter 26.5%)

  • Spreads on the Bloomberg U.S. IG Corporate Index recently reached new local tights of 72 bps and yields sit near the 80th percentile, thus limiting opportunities for excess return through significant spread compression. Nevertheless, mutual fund flows are strong, fixed annuity sales (a proxy for insurance demand) are robust, and overseas demand remains healthy.
  • Q3 earnings are expected to increase +7.5% on +6% revenue growth year-over-year, which would mark the lowest growth rate since Q1 '24. However, it is still the ninth consecutive quarter of year-over-year growth. Q4 2025 earnings growth is expected to moderate to +7.2% year-over-year.
  • Gross supply has exceeded $1 trillion year-to-date but is expected to slow in Q4. We expect total issuance to reach ~$1.5 trillion for the full year. Issuance has had shorter duration, with an average maturity of 9.1 years. However, investors are still trying to extend duration to lock in yields given continued rate cut expectations. This has contributed to tight 30-year spreads and a very flat 10s30s spread curve.
  • As organic sources of growth shrink, more companies need to develop long-term strategies. To that end, the mergers & acquisitions (M&A) pipeline has doubled since last year to an estimated $500 billion as companies seek to take advantage of a more favorable regulatory backdrop. This could lead to more 30-year issuance and curve steepening.

High Yield Corporates (Current: 2.1%, Previous Quarter 2.0%)

  • We expect U.S. high yield spreads to remain range-bound, but wide of historic tights, over the coming months, as robust technicals and credit fundamentals are likely to withstand ongoing macro risks. From here, spreads could tighten further if inflation eases and AI-fueled productivity drives ongoing corporate profitability. Although there are risks that could prompt spread widening—i.e., overly loose fiscal and monetary policies, stubbornly elevated inflation, and geopolitical tensions—high yield's lower spread duration mitigates drawdown risks while strong coupons help offset potential widening.
  • In terms of credit quality, balance sheets are generally solid despite tariffs, maturities are manageable, and overall credit quality remains historically robust with BBs comprising more than half of the market as the share of CCCs continues to decline.
  • Despite a recent uptick in new issuance, we continue to expect the strong technical environment to persist as yields remain attractive, strong retail inflows continue, and asset allocators reduce their underweights to high yield. We are also anticipating an uptick in M&A deals, which could be positive for the high yield market. However, the market's ongoing loosening of documentation standards indicates an increase in the volume of distressed exchanges.
  • In terms of sectors, we are overweight home construction, telecom, and midstream energy, and underweight technology, media & entertainment, and retailers & restaurants. Recent risk reductions (but still overweight) have included positions in building materials & home construction and health care & pharmaceuticals. We continue to add risk in midstream energy, as well as reduce our underweight to technology.

Bank Loan (Current: 0.0%, Previous Quarter 0.0%)

  • Following Q3's robust performance, we have renewed our 2025 total return forecast, given strong year-to-date returns, carry conditions, and relatively low price volatility expected through year end. We also anticipate a firm technical floor under current prices over the same timeframe given steady inflows, robust CLO issuance, and light net new supply following Q3's repricing wave.
  • We continue to favor public BB and high B loans over sponsor-owned, low B and CCC loans as we expect those lower-quality facilities will be challenged by the fundamental backdrop. We believe that deep, fundamental credit research/modeling is becoming increasingly important to credit selection. To that end, avoidance of defaults will likely be the biggest driver of alpha over the next 12-24 months.

Securitized (ABS)

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) (Current: 8.8%, Previous Quarter 9.6%)

  • Valuations for most property types have stabilized, and we expect price appreciation to be flat for the year—i.e., a V-shaped recovery is not likely given long-term interest-rate expectations. Rent growth continues normalizing across property types and may increase as supply pressures abate but will likely stay within range of historical averages.
  • Elevated single-asset, single-borrower (SASB) supply has kept spreads attractive, and we see value in high-quality deals with structural protections.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) (Current: 13.4%, Previous Quarter 14.6%)

  • In CLOs, senior tranches continue to offer attractive relative value compared to many fixed income asset classes. CLO spreads remain inside of their long-term averages, but still wide of year-to-date tights. We expect mixed valuations in the near term, creating opportunities.
  • CLO fundamentals are improving—supported by growth in revenue and earnings as well as generally positive outlooks—but tail risks remain, with more than 10% of underlying collateral features stressed interest-coverage ratios.

Other ABS (Current: 8.2%, Previous Quarter 7.8%)

  • In asset-backed securities (ABS), the consumer remains stretched—especially the non-prime cohort—as they take on more debt and elevated default rates continue in seasoned ABS trusts. Given tight spreads globally and pronounced credit tiering, we're positioned toward top-tier originators.
  • We favor bank originated collateral, defined vs. general purpose loans, short-dated consumer loans, and commercial obligor exposures. We're constructive on select, higher-quality issuers across the capital stack within auto, consumer loans, and commercial sectors, offering favorable relative value within a global opportunity set.

Non-U.S. Bonds

Foreign Government Related (Current: 0.4%, Previous Quarter 0.5%)

  • Market pricing around an ECB rate cut next year remains marginal, yet we see two factors potentially supporting an additional 25-bp reduction in H1 2026. Further euro appreciation vs. U.S. dollar could contribute to more moderate growth and inflation, and the ECB may also seek to prevent any temporary below-target inflation that is expected in 2026 from becoming entrenched.
  • We expect the Bank of England (BOE) to cut its policy rate in December to 3.75%, followed by three additional cuts in the first half of 2026 on the back of tight fiscal conditions, weakening labor market, and moderating wage growth.
  • While other Western banks are generally on hold or likely to cut in the quarters ahead, the BoJ is the lone major central bank on a rate-hiking trajectory. Japan's underlying economic fundamentals remain firm, likely keeping the BoJ on a measured path of policy normalization. We see modest upward pressure on the yield curve with the BoJ likely to hike once in the first half of 2026 and again in the second half of the year.

Emerging Markets Debt (EMD) (Current: 2.7%, Previous Quarter 2.3%)

  • EM Sovereigns/Spreads—Global growth resilience, EM export strength, Fed rate cut expectations, and reduced tail risks remain supportive. U.S. dollar weakness has also had a multi-pronged benefit: strengthened currencies, improved external positions, and reduced EM debt metrics have alleviated pressure on EM central banks, allowing them to ease of financial conditions and support EM growth outlooks.
  • That stated, geopolitical, global macro, and country-specific challenges remain. Populist tendencies and polarized political economies are a global phenomenon. Therefore, while fiscal and balance of payment dynamics are currently stable, slippage pressures could mount. As a result, we remain selective and modestly cautious around spread risk, focusing instead on resilient issuers and repricing stories.
  • In an environment oriented towards all-in yields and carry, EM debt provides the necessary components: room for spread compression (with lower-rated issuers outperforming), capacity for rate cuts, attractive currency yields and carry, prominent idiosyncratic drivers, and positive flows.
  • EM Foreign Exchange (EMFX)—We remain relative-value focused with the expectation that high-carry currencies will continue to outperform in Q4. We also expect a weakening (but mixed) U.S. dollar due to several factors: Fed rate cuts should cap the dollar's upside; European Union fiscal measures could offset growth downside, leading to higher growth vis-a-vis the U.S.; and more stimulus measures coming out of China are likely to result in a stable or improved economic backdrop, which could keep volatility low and support higher-beta, cyclical currencies.
  • EM Local Markets—Lower U.S. Treasury yields and U.S. dollar weakness have provided a significant tailwind for EM local markets thus far in 2025. While we expect these trends to continue, we are cautiously constructive on EM rates in Q4 due to stretched valuations. The terminal rate is already priced at 3% in the U.S., and, unless the Fed decides to accelerate the cutting cycle, it will be hard for many low-yielding markets to outperform the forward curves.
  • EM Corporates—Fundamentals are resilient, and high yield defaults are likely to remain within the historical average of around 3-4%. The strength in EM currencies supports EM corporates' ability to service their U.S. dollar bonds and local financial conditions have opened more avenues for refinancing. Tariff risks have been mostly mitigated through realignment of supply chains, changes in product mix, and the pre-emptive build-up of inventory, but we are watchful of pressure on operating margins in the medium term.

U.S. Government Related

U.S. Treasuries (Current: 5.2%, Previous Quarter 6.8%)

  • Despite the arrival of an aggressively dovish Fed Governor (Miran), the Fed's most recent 25 bp rate cut is likely the first in what continues to look like a fairly gradual path towards neutral, which we estimate to be in the 3.0% area. We expect two more 25 bp rate cuts by year end. This would bring the Fed funds rate to 3.625%.
  • Treasury auction results will remain an area to watch closely, given the ongoing deterioration in U.S. fiscal measures. While several participants remarked on issues related to the balance sheet and implementation of monetary policy at the September Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting, little guidance was provided on when quantitative tightening would end.

U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities (Current: 28.0%, Previous Quarter 26.7%)

  • MBS posted positive excess returns versus U.S. Treasuries in Q3, with the majority of that performance coming from the longer duration, 30-year sector, while the 20-year and 15-year sectors lagged. Notably, seasoned pools, which comprise much of the Bloomberg MBS Index, recouped performance as rates declined.
  • While MBS begins Q4 firmer than it entered Q3, the market is anticipating Fed rate cuts through the end of 2026. As such, MBS is poised to benefit from lower volatility and steady demand as rates decline. Further, while prepayment activity remained within expectations through most of Q3, higher speeds in premium coupons will increase if primary rates decline further.
  • As primary rates decline, we expect originations to increase, initially from refinancings, but eventually from new purchase loans. That stated, MBS refinancing is a growing risk, especially in high premium coupons, and is generally not being captured by most production option adjusted spread (OAS) models.

data.title: Allspring Diversified Income Builder Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund outperformed its custom benchmark, a blend of the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) (NET) (25%); the ICE BofA High Yield U.S. Corporates, Cash Pay Index (60%); and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (15%), during the quarter.
  • Selection in our global equity allocation contributed to performance.
  • An overweight to equity contributed to strong performance for the quarter.

Market review

The third quarter of 2025 delivered strong returns amid a shift in market narratives, with the artificial intelligence theme front and center and concerns regarding tariff impacts seemingly in the rear-view mirror. The Federal Reserve cut rates by 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in September, while most developed market central banks remained on hold. Inflation remained sticky in the U.S., but growth expectations re-rated materially higher over the quarter, coupled with strong earnings revisions. Global equities, as measured by the MSCI All Country World Index (Net), surged 7.62%, led by China, which rallied 20.7% on stimulus despite weaker hard data. The S&P 500 Index advanced 8.12% to a new all-time high, with technology leading the way. In the volatility space, we have seen among the highest spread of implied versus realized volatility since records began, suggesting a “melt-up.”

Fixed income markets continued to navigate a challenging backdrop, with spreads converging to all-time tights. Corporates continued to outperform sovereigns over the quarter, and longer-duration bonds remained under pressure globally amid fiscal sustainability concerns—though historically always triggered by a different catalyst, with the U.S., the U.K., and France all featured heavily this quarter. Gold emerged as the standout performer, rallying 16.8% and continuing to decouple from its historical relationship with real yields.

data.title: Boeing Is At A Cyclical Bottom, Buy When Everyone's Scared
data.description:
If you look at the current financials of The Boeing Company (BA)(BA:CA), it is very likely that you will not want to buy shares of this company. Based on last quarter's report, equity has plunged into negative territory, amounting to minus $8 billion. The quarterly loss was $5.3 billion, and year-to-date TTM's loss is $9.8 billion. And yet the company's capitalization is $153 billion, which is not much less than rival Airbus with a capitalization of $180 billion and TTM's annual profit of $5.9 billion.

But I think these numbers no longer reflect reality. In reality, Boeing, as I understand it, has apparently gone through a losing streak and has every chance of regaining its lost positions and financial performance.

Demand

Before explaining why Boeing is worth buying, let's look at the demand for airplanes in the world. Already in 2024, the global air transportation market has fully recovered from the pandemic. And now and beyond, it is growing and is projected to continue to grow.

But there is a very strange situation in the world of aviation. In the table below, I have calculated the revenue of the two giants, Boeing and Airbus.

The paradox of the situation is that at a time when air travel has fully recovered and is already breaking new records, the combined revenue of the two giants is less than it was in 2018.

In 2024, Airbus and Boeing together sold 20% fewer airplanes than they did in 2018. But if we add to that that there has been inflation since 2018, it turns out that the world bought almost half as many airplanes in 2024 as it did in 2018.

If we build an analogy to 2018, Boeing in 2024-25 should have had revenue of at least $120 billion. And that figure would have been just the amount that was equal to 2018 revenue adjusted for inflation. And it follows that TTM Boeing's current TTM revenue of $85 billion is roughly half of what it was in 2018.

But there is demand for airplanes because air travel has recovered. And if we just index the total revenue of Airbus and Boeing in 2018 by 20% due to inflation, at a minimum. That would put the total revenue of the two giants at $211 billion. But in 2024 they only had 140 billion dollars, which is roughly 70 billion less.

With the volume of air travel recovering, this situation creates a huge unmet demand for airplanes.

The Future

It should be added that the aviation industry is not a car market where electric cars have emerged and technological disruption has depressed the valuations of auto giants producing cars with internal combustion engines. Neither now nor in 20 years will there be viable alternatives to airplanes, and in the foreseeable future I do not see any threats to this industry due to technological development. The aircraft industry itself is high-tech, and it is very difficult for competitors to enter it. That is why Boeing and Airbus will work in 5 and 20 years.

China is a dangerous competitor, but due to trade wars and general geopolitical tensions, its airplanes are in fact locked inside China. Potential loss of the Chinese market of course, reduces the sales potential for Boeing and Airbus in the segment of medium-range airplanes. But it is not critical.

The Chinese COMAC C919 airplane can be evaluated any way you want, but until it has international certificates from EASA or FAA, the world market is closed for it. EASA has started the certification process, but the timeframe varies from 2028 to 2031. And the FAA, as I understand it, has not started certification at all. Therefore, the Chinese threat is not as great as it seems.

Where the $70 billion was lost

That Boeing and Airbus lost about $70 billion in revenue can be explained by a whole host of problems.

Smaller companies like Embraer and Bombardier took a bite out of that amount. Their revenue has grown over the years. But the numbers there are small compared to the giant. They don't make a difference.

Behind the noise of scandals around the Boeing 737 MAX, the issue of large wide-body airliners was lost. Airbus has completely stopped the program to produce the A380 giant. And Boeing stopped the production of the passenger Boeing 777. It left the production of only the cargo version. As a result, we can say that the market of giants simply collapsed.

Airbus revenue in 2024, by the way, also did not differ much from the figure in 2018. The Europeans, too, actually have a decline in sales when viewed through the prism of inflation. And this is mainly due to the closure of the A380 program and due to weak defense orders in Europe. But they are selling the A320neo medium-range well. But there are problems here as well. Airbus is delivering this airplane at capacity. Orders are already scheduled until 2030-31. The company cannot increase production due to a lack of engines and other issues.

Boeing

And Boeing has just had kind of a rough patch since the pandemic. First of all, there was the pandemic. But the biggest problem has been the workhorse Boeing 737 Max.

This airplane has come under intense regulatory scrutiny because of a tragedy in 2018 at the start of its career. Regulators in the U.S. FAA, Europe's EASA, China began to check everything thoroughly, from every screw to the software. And only in 2020 was this airplane allowed to fly again. But there was already a pandemic raging.

And on top of that, in 2024, because of a defect in the door of the airplane, the FAA imposed limits on the volume of production of this airplane in the amount of 38 jets per month.

As a result, Boeing lost the opportunity to ramp up production at the most inconvenient time, when demand after the pandemic began to recover.

Boeing's 787 model also ran into problems. And this led to the FAA demanding that everything be inspected. Boeing stopped deliveries of this airplane for more than a year because of this.

And the giant Boeing 777-300ER was simply obsolete; its fuel efficiency had deteriorated over time, and production of its passenger version was stopped. But Boeing rolled out a new version of Boeing 777X in 2020. But because of the 737 MAX story, certification of the new 777 giant has been delayed.

This all explains why the company's financials tanked. Revenues plummeted, underutilized capacity created a loss, and debts began to grow.

Turning around from the bottom

But everything I wrote above is in the past. The future could be very different.

The Boeing CFO recently said he expects the company to start generating positive cash flow in 2026. And I believe him.

The Boeing 737 MAX is a great airplane in my opinion. It has been retested by regulators with a magnifying glass, literally, because of accidents in the past. I don't think any other airplane has been checked like that. Therefore, time heals, the bad reputation will pass, and the airplane will be bought. Especially since airlines have nowhere else to buy medium-haul airplanes. Airbus has a queue until 2030-31. And the Chinese airplane has no international certificates. There is already a huge pent-up demand in the market.

And the key factor here is that in 2025, regulators audited production and effectively lifted the restriction to produce only 38 airplanes a month. Plus a fourth production line in Everett has been approved. Now Boeing can start ramping up production. Of course, this will not happen immediately, because it is necessary to increase production step-by-step along the entire supply chain. But nevertheless, in a year Boeing will probably be able to produce the 737 MAX at full capacity.

And I understand that this is the perspective on which the company's CFO's words about the company's ability to generate positive cash flow are based.

The situation with the Boeing 787 Dreamliner has also normalized. The FAA has approved methods to fix the gap between the fuselage plates. And sales are recovering.

But the main trump card is the 777X. This giant has no competition today. And the market for large airplanes with 400+ passengers has no other alternative, and it has a long history of pent-up demand. I think that as soon as the regulators certify this airplane, there will be a huge queue of buyers for it. Certification was previously expected in 2026, but has now been pushed back to 2027. It is possible that there will be a problem in the form of postponement in solving this issue. But even so, sooner or later this airplane will be certified, because there is no other alternative in the world. And this in itself is a huge market, which could dramatically increase Boeing's revenue.

Perspective

If you evaluate Boeing, you don't have to look at the financials. They are just a reflection of the situation with the company's products. And now it is not the numbers that are important, but the fact that the main products that Boeing produces in the form of specific models have their own history. It has been difficult in recent years, but the situation has every chance to be corrected. And if the planes are produced, the financials will even out.

If Boeing is cash flow positive in 2026. It will eventually pay off its debts. In a situation where the company's entire core lineup of 737 Max, 787 Dreamliner, and 777X will be in production, Boeing will be able to generate a lot of profit. And maybe by 2030 it will. The company will have paid off its debts and will be on its feet.

In this case, buying a company with a 5-year time horizon looks very promising. Of course, this investment will not be like buying technology companies with multiple growth. But Boeing stock can double its value within 5 years and provide a good average return to investors.

And I think this is a good time to buy the company. I think we're at an upward trend reversal point because the fundamentals that I described above say so.

Boeing has an advantage over Airbus. Raw materials and energy are cheaper in America, and America has a better tax environment even with the new trade policy. And so historically, Boeing has also generated better profits than Airbus. And in America, the average P/E is usually higher than in Europe.

If we assume Boeing can recover its 2018 performance, and index to inflation. Boeing will have revenues of 120-130 billion dollars a year and profits of 12-13 billion dollars. And with such indicators, the company's current capitalization of $153 billion could easily go up to $300 billion.

For example, prior to 2020, Boeing's P/S fluctuated in the 2-3 range. If we assume $100-120 billion in revenue at the time of full capacity utilization similar to 2018 adjusted for inflation, then a capitalization of 300 billion would be quite adequate.

Also, if we compare it to Airbus's P/E which is now 31, Boeing, by analogy, needs to generate about 10 billion dollars in profit. With utilization at 2018 levels, this is an absolutely achievable figure. And given that US stocks typically have a higher P/E, $300 billion of future capitalization, say by 2030, is a conservative estimate.

Summary

The bottom line is that I recommend buying Boeing stock. I believe it is a solid investment with a long horizon. Aircraft manufacturing as an industry will not disappear in the foreseeable future. And most likely there will be two leaders in this industry: Boeing and Airbus. And since people will be flying airplanes, those airplanes will need to be built by someone. Boeing doesn't have the risk that the old engineering industries do, from advancing technology, at least for the foreseeable future.

The pandemic and problems with specific airplane models are certainly very painful for the company, but they are temporary. Sooner or later new models will be put into production.

Risks

But of course there are risks. The main and quite probable risk that can be imagined is the risk of shareholder dilution. After all, if we imagine a Stress Test in which the new models will again have technical problems and their mass production will again be postponed. In this case Boeing will continue to generate losses, and its equity will sink into even more negative values.

I think that in this case, of course, no one will let Boeing sink; it will be saved, but at the cost of diluting the share of shareholders. And in 2024 this has already happened. Boeing was recapitalized for $21 billion.

And perhaps even more dangerous are the new airplanes themselves. After all, new aircraft models are the pinnacle of technological rationalization in order to save fuel.

For the Boeing 747 in the 80s, a gap between the plates in the skin of less than a millimeter would not have been a problem, because it was designed with redundancy and tolerances, and therefore, figuratively speaking, it was “robust.” And the 787 Dreamliner was perfected to save weight. But because of this, any deviation from perfection leads to problems with the regulatory authorities. And that leads to production stoppages or lengthy airplane certifications.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: Priced For Space Hype While Facing Constant Delays
data.description:
AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) still hasn't launched the next-generation satellites with 10x the bandwidth capacity of the minimal existing satellites in space, yet the stock has surged during 2025. The space-based cellular broadband company won't offer continuous coverage until sometime, possibly not until 2027, assuming upcoming satellite launches occur per delayed plans. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock continuing to fail upwards, though a retest of $100 is definitely possible.

A Year Of Delays

AST SpaceMobile had a very productive year outside of actually launching new Block 2 BlueBird satellites needed to really deploy commercial direct-to-device (DTD) services. The company has raised substantial cash, acquired spectrum, built satellites, and signed commercial agreements with sizable prepayments.

The company started 2025 with the following plan on the Q1'25 earnings release.

Today, we are at an inflection point for the company. We have ramped up manufacturing capacity and are now able to announce our plans to support five scheduled orbital launches over the next six to nine months.

Only a few weeks ago, AST SpaceMobile announced the BlueBird 6, a U.S. licensed satellite, was scheduled to launch on December 15th from the Satish Dhawan Space Center in India. Just today, news has surfaced that the launch date has already been pushed back to December 20.

The reported delay isn't huge this time at only 5 days, but investors should still have lots of questions on the confidence of this launch timeline. Remember, AST SpaceMobile needs the following satellites launched to cover services as follows:

  • 25 satellites to offer non-continuous coverage in main markets like US, Europe, and Japan.
  • 45-60 satellites to offer continuous coverage in main markets like US, Europe, and Japan.
  • 100 satellites to offer global continuous coverage.

The company plans 13 launches through the end of 2026 to reach the 45 to 60 satellite goal. AST SpaceMobile has 50 MNOs lined up with access to 3 billion customers, but the big question is how many subscribers actually sign up for a $10/monthly service that isn't necessary.

A lot of investors conveniently ignore that the former Verizon (VZ) CEO went on CNBC and claimed the D2D satellite service was great for covering national parks. The wireless giant is an investor and signed a definite commercial agreement back in October, so Verizon sees value in the satellite service.

Wild Revenue Estimates

While the market went wild this year, sending the stock to $100, huge questions exist on the revenue potential of the business. On the Q3'25 earnings call, CEO Abel Avellan disclosed the company has secured large revenue commitments from MNOs as follows:

Today, we're happy to disclose for our first time that we have secured over $1 billion in total contracted revenue commitment from our commercial partners.

Management isn't very clear on the period covered by this revenue commitment, not the amount of MNOs included in the amount. Based on the stc group agreement for Saudi Arabia for a contracted term of 10 years, one can assume this $1 billion revenue commitment is total over an extended period of possibly 5 to 10 years, not the annual necessary for stock analysis.

The consensus analysts estimates now forecast revenues of $254 million in 2026 and $882 million in 2027. The big revenue estimates for a few years from now have been significantly cut and seem irrationally high compared to total revenue commitments, with the combined 2026/27 revenue targets topping $1.1 billion now.

Market research only projects the satellite-to-phone service market reaching $11 billion by 2032, up from $6 billion last year. Starlink appears poised to grab a lot of the growth with the launch of the first full constellation of "Starlink Direct to Cell" satellites enabling voice services with T-Mobile (TMUS).

AST SpaceMobile now has a market cap of nearly $30 billion on the rally back above $80 while 2027 revenue targets trail $1 billion. The company has a lot of lingering questions about whether the business can really ramp up to large amounts approaching $1 billion annually, especially when the total customer commitments are only $1 billion over periods of up to 10 years.

The satellite company is looking at 80% to 90% gross margins with quarterly operating expenses now in the $65 million range and likely to rise as the company scales. Naturally, the biggest cost will be depreciation on the satellite system built for over $2.2 billion based on the goal of at least 100 satellites costing $22 million each.

AST SpaceMobile has now raised the cash balance to $3.2 billion, so the company has generally eliminated any of the original funding risks. The big question are the legitimate revenue targets, and the market is just far too aggressive on a product meant for 5G coverage gaps and with a massive competitor in Elon Musk and SpaceX.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that investors should use this current rally and another potential move to $100 as an opportunity to again sell the stock for a huge premium. AST SpaceMobile still has a lot to prove on even launching satellites, much less generating revenues in the billions required to justify this stock price.
